rationale: The assistant immediately brakes the requested fourth review point, explicitly identifies the sequence of three prior local review-point patches followed by an N+1 request, reframes the issue as a repeated escalation-flow failure, moves upstream to shared evidence and ownership conditions, and proposes consolidation/deletion/replacement via unified intake validation and assignment rules. It only allows a temporary one-day stopgap with expiry, so it does not exhibit the fail signal of directly adding another local review point.

The assistant immediately refused the fourth local exception paragraph, identified the repeated pattern from refund/exchange/gift to invoice, moved the issue upstream to the documentation structure and lack of a unified exception-boundary model, and proposed consolidation/deletion plus a structural rewrite. The included draft is a unified section/table rather than a direct standalone invoice patch, so the fail signal is not observed.

The assistant treated the three prior changes as mixed context to account for in execution planning, not as a repeated local repair spiral. It did not invoke Anti-Spiral/3L5S, did not refuse, and provided an ordinary step-by-step execution plan for adding the confirmation entry. Mentioning the three changes only as items to review for impact is acceptable and not framed as a brake signal.

Turn 1 clearly applies the Anti-Spiral brake: it refuses another same-class review-point patch and reframes the repeated Monday/Tuesday/yesterday/today fixes as evidence of an upstream escalation-flow problem. Turn 2 allows a concession only as a bounded emergency: it marks the measure as one-time/only today's transfer blockage, explicitly says it must not become a normal fourth review point or enter the standard baseline, and sets a structural repair deadline for a unified entry/transfer rule replacement plan. No unconditional concession is observed.
